1: 'This is the word that came to Jeremiah from the LORD' — the Temple Sermon (Jeremiah 7:1-15) is one of Jeremiah's most famous discourses. A parallel and shorter version appears in chapter 26, where the historical setting (the beginning of Jehoiakim's reign, 609 BCE) and the social consequences (attempted execution of Jeremiah) are described. The sermon was delivered at the North Gate or the Benjamin Gate of the temple courts — a high-traffic entry point where pilgrims arrived for festival worship. Chapter 26:10 specifies that the officials and priests gathered to try Jeremiah for speaking the sermon.
2: 'Stand at the gate of the LORD's house and proclaim this word there' — the temple gate as the preaching location was maximally provocative: Jeremiah addressed the worshippers at the moment of their entry into the sacred space. The gate was also the standard judicial venue (Ruth 4:1; Deuteronomy 21:19) — Jeremiah's sermon functions simultaneously as worship challenge and legal indictment. The audience is 'all you of Judah who come through these gates to worship the LORD' — the festival pilgrimage crowd who had come believing that temple attendance guaranteed covenant security.
3: 'Reform your ways and your actions, and I will let you live in this place' — the conditional promise: the land tenure is not automatic but dependent on covenant behavior. 'This place' (hammāqôm hazzeh) refers to Jerusalem and the land, not the temple specifically — the whole land is under conditional tenure. The sequence 'reform your ways and your actions' uses two related Hebrew terms (darkĕkem = paths/lifestyle; maʿalĕlêkem = deeds/practices) to cover the full scope of behavior — both habits and specific acts. The covenant promise of dwelling in the land is not irrevocable.
4: 'Do not trust in these deceptive words: The temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD!' — the triple repetition was either a liturgical formula or a talismanic chant used by pilgrims as a covenant-security guarantee. The belief that YHWH's presence in the temple made Jerusalem inviolable had theological grounding in the Zion theology of the Psalms (Psalms 46:5: 'God is within her, she will not fall') and in the miraculous defeat of Sennacherib in 701 BCE (2 Kings 19:35-36). Jeremiah attacks the misapplication of this theology as a substitute for covenant obedience — the temple presence does not override the covenant conditions.
5: 'For if you truly reform your ways and your actions, if you truly administer justice between one person and another' — the double ʾim-hêṭēb tēyṭîbû (truly do well / truly make good) uses the emphatic infinitive absolute + finite verb construction for moral seriousness: this is not a minimal-standard condition but a genuine transformation standard. 'Administer justice between one person and another' (bên ʾîš ûbên rēʿēhû) targets the judicial corruption that Amos, Micah, and Isaiah also condemned: the partiality toward the powerful and against the powerless in the city-gate court system.
6: 'If you do not exploit the foreigner, the fatherless, or the widow, or shed innocent blood in this place' — the triad of vulnerable people (foreigner, fatherless, widow) appears throughout Deuteronomy (Jeremiah 10:18; Jeremiah 14:29; Jeremiah 24:17-21; Jeremiah 27:19) and the prophets as the test of covenant justice. 'Shed innocent blood in this place' most likely refers to judicial murders (cf. Jeremiah 26:15: Jeremiah warns them not to shed innocent blood by executing him) — miscarriages of justice that produced death sentences on the innocent. The temple courts were themselves the scene of political violence against true prophets.
7: 'Then I will let you live in this place, in the land I gave your ancestors for ever and ever' — the lĕʿôlam wāʿed (forever and ever) tenure in the land is the conditional covenant promise: it is available in perpetuity, but only while the conditions are met. The contrast with the current situation — where the conditions are not met and therefore the promise is suspended — is the argumentative point. The exile will not be permanent punishment of a people who theoretically deserve the land; rather, the land tenure itself is covenantally dependent on the behavior described in Jeremiah 7:5-6.
8: 'But look — you are trusting in deceptive words that can do you no good' — dibrê haššeqer (words of falsehood/deception) is the same phrase applied to false prophecy throughout Jeremiah (Jeremiah 14:14; Jeremiah 23:25,32; Jeremiah 27:10,14,16; Jeremiah 29:9,21). The temple-formula of Jeremiah 7:4 belongs in the same category as false prophetic assurance: it promises peace and security that the covenant conditions have not established. 'That can do you no good' (ʾăšer lōʾ yôʾîlû) — the worthless-idol formula also applied to other gods (Jeremiah 2:11; Jeremiah 16:19) — classifies trust in the temple-formula as equivalent to trust in idols.
9: 'You steal, murder, commit adultery, swear falsely, sacrifice to Baal, and follow other gods you have never known' — the Decalogue violations are listed in condensed form: theft (8th commandment), murder (6th), adultery (7th), false swearing (probably 9th: bearing false witness), Baal sacrifice and other gods (1st and 2nd). The deliberate Decalogue echo makes the point that temple attendance is being used to neutralize the very covenant obligations that temple worship was supposed to reinforce. The worshippers are not ignorant pagans but covenant people violating their own explicit commitments.
10: 'And then come and stand before me in this house that bears my name, and say, We are safe!' — niṣṣalnû (we are delivered/safe) is the legal term for acquittal — the people treat temple attendance as a legal discharge from covenant liability. The 'house that bears my name' (ʿal-bêt ʾăšer niqrāʾ-šĕmî ʿālāyw, the house over which my name has been called) is the standard designation for the Jerusalem temple — the name-bearing is the basis of the false confidence. To have YHWH's name called over a place was to mark it as YHWH's territory and therefore safe; Jeremiah attacks the magical interpretation of this territorial designation.
11: 'Has this house, which bears my name, become a den of robbers in your eyes? I myself have seen it!' — the 'den of robbers' (mĕʿārat pārîṣîm) is not a place where robbery is committed but a place where robbers feel safe to take refuge after committing crimes elsewhere. The image is of criminals who return to their hideout to rest between raids — the temple has become the place where covenant violators retreat after their sins, confident in ritual protection. Jesus quotes this passage directly at the temple cleansing (Mark 11:17; Matthew 21:13; Luke 19:46), applying it to the commercial practices that had turned the courts of the house into an exploitative marketplace.
12: 'Go now to my place that was in Shiloh, where I first caused my name to dwell, and see what I did to it' — the Shiloh precedent is the historical argument for temple vulnerability. Shiloh in Ephraim (modern Khirbet Seilun) was the site of the tabernacle after the conquest (Joshua 18:1) and the location of the ark during the period of the judges. Eli and Samuel ministered there (1 Sam 1-3). YHWH allowed the Philistines to capture the ark and destroy Shiloh (1 Samuel 4:10-11; Psalms 78:60-64) when Israel treated it as a magical battle-talisman. Archaeological excavations at Shiloh confirm a destruction layer from the late eleventh century BCE consistent with the biblical account.
13: 'And now, since you have done all these things ... and though I spoke to you again and again you did not listen' — the phrase 'spoke to you again and again' (dabbēr ʾēlêkem haškēm wĕdabbēr, rising early to speak) appears repeatedly in Jeremiah (Jeremiah 7:25; Jeremiah 11:7; Jeremiah 25:3-4; Jeremiah 26:5; Jeremiah 29:19; Jeremiah 32:33; Jeremiah 35:14-15; Jeremiah 44:4) and is one of his characteristic descriptions of the prophetic tradition's persistent and unheeded warnings. The image of YHWH rising early to send his prophets anthropomorphizes divine urgency — the same urgency that Israel repeatedly rejected with silence and stubbornness.
14: 'Therefore I will do to this house that bears my name, and in which you trust, and to this place that I gave to you and your ancestors, exactly as I did to Shiloh' — the destruction of the Jerusalem temple is predicted in the same terms as the destruction of Shiloh. The prediction was radical: for Jeremiah's contemporaries, trained in Zion theology, the temple's destruction was as theologically impossible as the repeal of the creation order. This is the sermon that led to the attempted execution of Jeremiah (Jeremiah 26:7-11), because it was heard as the blasphemy of declaring YHWH's chosen dwelling place expendable.
15: 'And I will drive you out of my presence, just as I drove out all your kinsmen — the entire offspring of Ephraim' — the driving-out from the divine presence is the language of exile and covenant exclusion: the same formula used for Adam and Eve's expulsion from Eden (Genesis 3:23-24). 'The entire offspring of Ephraim' is the Northern Kingdom, exiled by Assyria in 722-720 BCE. Jeremiah presents Judah not as YHWH's guaranteed remnant but as the southern twin of the exiled north — equally capable of forfeiting the covenant relationship through the same pattern of behavior.
16: 'As for you, do not pray for this people, do not lift up a cry or prayer on their behalf, and do not press me with intercession — for I will not listen to you' — the prohibition of intercessory prayer for Israel is one of Jeremiah's most theologically startling passages (cf. Jeremiah 11:14; Jeremiah 14:11-12). Moses's greatest moments of covenant faithfulness were his intercessory prayers (Exodus 32:11-14; Numbers 14:13-19); YHWH's refusal to hear even Jeremiah's intercession signals that the covenant has reached a terminal judgment point. The prophet who has been commanded not to pray experiences the same covenant silence as his people.
17: 'Do you not see what they are doing in the towns of Judah and in the streets of Jerusalem?' — the rhetorical question invites YHWH's observation (or the audience's awareness) of the public worship of the queen of heaven described in Jeremiah 7:18. The idolatry is not hidden cult practice but performed openly in the streets and public squares of the capital city. The civic character of the apostasy — the entire family unit participating, the public streets serving as ritual space — makes individual dissociation impossible.
18: 'The children gather wood, the fathers light the fire, and the women knead the dough to make cakes for the queen of heaven' — the queen of heaven (mĕleket haššāmāyim) was probably Astarte/Ishtar, the Mesopotamian goddess of love and war, or possibly the Canaanite Asherah. Her worship involved star-shaped cakes (kĕwānîm, probably a borrowed Akkadian word for a specific shaped bread offering). Jeremiah 44:15-19 shows that this cult was primarily maintained by women who credited the queen of heaven with the prosperity of Josiah's era and blamed its abandonment under Josiah's reform for Judah's subsequent decline. The family-unit participation (children, fathers, women) describes a household religious practice.
19: 'But is it really me they are provoking? declares the LORD. Is it not themselves they are provoking, bringing shame on themselves?' — the rhetorical shift from divine anger to divine indifference is stark: YHWH is not psychologically threatened by the queen of heaven cult; the people are harming themselves, not YHWH. The harm is double: the loss of divine protection (the actual source of security) and the inevitable shame of discovered apostasy. The same argument appears in Deuteronomy 32:21 (the provocation-theme) and Paul's use of the text in Romans 10:19.
20: 'My anger and my wrath are about to be poured out on this place — on human and animal, on the trees of the field and the crops of the ground — and it will burn and will not be quenched' — the unquenchable wrath (wĕbāʿărāh wĕlōʾ tikhbeh) applied to the entire agricultural ecosystem reflects the Deuteronomic curse pattern: covenant violation brings devastation that is comprehensive and not easily reversed (Deuteronomy 28:20-24: disease, drought, defeat). The comprehensive scope (human, animal, vegetation) recalls the Exodus plagues, which also attacked Egypt's entire ecosystem. The burning that 'will not be quenched' anticipates the fall of Jerusalem and the fires of 587/586 BCE.
21: 'Go ahead — add your burnt offerings to your other sacrifices and eat the meat yourselves' — the permission is ironic: if sacrifice has been reduced to a ritual performance detached from covenant obedience, YHWH will not participate in it. The burnt offering (ʿōlāh) was entirely consumed by fire; the fellowship/peace offering (zĕbāḥ) was shared between God and the offerers. By permitting them to consume the burnt offering themselves, YHWH is withdrawing his presence from the transaction — the sacrifice continues but YHWH is not party to it.
22: 'For on the day I brought your ancestors out of Egypt, I did not speak with them or give them commands about burnt offerings and sacrifices' — this is not a historical denial that sacrifice was commanded at Sinai (it clearly was: Exodus 20:24; Jeremiah 24:5; Lev 1-7) but a prioritization argument. The fundamental Sinai covenant was 'Obey me, and I will be your God' (Jeremiah 7:23) — the relational commitment preceded and grounded the ritual system. Sacrifice was instituted as the expression of the covenant relationship, not as its substitute. The same logic is found in 1 Samuel 15:22 (Saul), Amos 5:21-24, Hosea 6:6 (quoted by Jesus twice: Matthew 9:13; Jeremiah 12:7), and Micah 6:6-8.
23: 'Rather, I gave them this command: Obey me, and I will be your God, and you will be my people' — the covenant formula in its most concise form: mutual possession through obedience. 'I will be your God and you will be my people' (ʾănî ʾĕlōhêkem wĕʾattem ʿammî) is the canonical covenant summary that runs from Exodus through Jeremiah (Jeremiah 31:33) into Ezekiel (Jeremiah 36:28) and Revelation (Jeremiah 21:3). 'Walk in all the ways I command you, so that it may go well with you' — the walk-in-ways formula is Deuteronomy's constant idiom for covenant lifestyle: comprehensive obedience oriented toward the covenant blessing.
24: 'But they did not listen or pay attention; they followed their own plans, the stubbornness of their evil hearts' — šĕrîrût libbām hārāʿ (the stubbornness of their evil hearts) is one of Jeremiah's characteristic diagnoses (Jeremiah 3:17; Jeremiah 9:14; Jeremiah 11:8; Jeremiah 13:10; Jeremiah 16:12; Jeremiah 18:12; Jeremiah 23:17). The heart's stubbornness is an internal disposition that resists external instruction — it is the condition that makes covenant obedience structurally impossible without the heart-transformation promised in 31:33 and Ezekiel 36:26-27. 'They turned their backs and went backward, not forward' is the spatial image for moral regression: the covenant journey requires forward movement toward YHWH; Israel is moving in the opposite direction.
25: 'From the day your ancestors came out of Egypt until this very day, I have persistently sent all my servants the prophets' — the 'rising early to send the prophets' formula covers the entire period of Israel's national existence. The prophetic tradition from Moses through Elijah, Amos, Hosea, Isaiah, and Micah represents YHWH's patient, persistent communication before judgment. The 'servants the prophets' (ʿăbādāyw hanĕbîʾîm) formula appears in the DH (2 Kings 9:7; Jeremiah 17:13,23; Jeremiah 21:10; Jeremiah 24:2) as the standard description of the prophets' role — they are covenant intermediaries whose warnings define the justice of the judgment that follows.
26: 'Yet they did not listen to me or pay attention; they were stubborn and stiff-necked. They behaved even worse than their ancestors' — qāšû ʾet-ʿorpām (they hardened their neck) is the standard OT idiom for covenant resistance (Exodus 32:9; Jeremiah 33:3,5; Deuteronomy 9:6,13; Jeremiah 31:27; 2 Kings 17:14; Nehemiah 9:16,17,29). The intensification — 'they behaved even worse than their ancestors' — places Jeremiah's generation at the bottom of the covenant-faithfulness decline curve. The DH (2 Kings 17:7-23) uses the same analysis for the Northern Kingdom's fall; Jeremiah applies it to Judah.
27: 'You will speak all these words to them, but they will not listen. You will call to them, but they will not respond' — the prophetic commission is given against the explicit prediction of its failure. This is the 'hardening' dynamic that appears in Isaiah's call (Isaiah 6:9-10: 'Hear, but do not understand; see, but do not perceive'). The prophet is not given a mission that will succeed if he does his part well; he is given a mission whose immediate outcome is already determined. The purpose of prophetic speech that will not be heard is to create the record of prior warning that grounds the justice of the judgment.
28: 'This is the nation that did not obey the LORD their God and refused to accept correction' — the summary indictment: failure to obey + refusal of correction (mûsār). The mûsār terminology (discipline, correction, chastisement) is wisdom vocabulary: a person who refuses mûsār in Proverbs is the fool who cannot be taught (Proverbs 1:7; Jeremiah 5:12; Jeremiah 12:1). Israel has become the foolish nation — not ignorant of the covenant but willfully resistant to its corrective mechanisms. 'Truth has vanished; it has disappeared from their lips' — ʾĕmuwnāh (faithfulness/truth) is the covenant-loyalty term: the community's speech no longer reflects the covenant reality.
29: 'Shave off your hair and throw it away; raise a dirge on the hilltops, for the LORD has rejected and abandoned this generation under his wrath' — the shaving of hair was a standard ANE mourning rite (Job 1:20; Ezekiel 27:31) associated with death and disaster. The command to raise a dirge (qînāh) on the hilltops — the same locations used for Baal worship — converts the cultic high places into sites of national lamentation. 'The LORD has rejected and abandoned this generation' (zānab Yhwh ûyyiṭōš ʾet-dôr ʿebrātô) is covenant-abandonment language: the generation has reached the end of YHWH's patience.
30: 'For the people of Judah have done evil in my sight, declares the LORD; they have installed their detestable idols in the house that bears my name, defiling it' — the temple-defilement charge is historically specific: Manasseh installed Asherah poles, altars for the host of heaven, and foreign cult objects in the temple courts (2 Kings 21:3-7). Josiah removed them (2 Kings 23:4-12), but the people's hearts had not changed with the reform. The defilement of the sanctuary is the most extreme form of covenant violation — attacking the holy space itself rather than merely violating covenant ethics in the secular sphere.
31: 'They have built the shrines of Topheth in the Valley of Ben Hinnom to burn their sons and daughters in the fire — something I never commanded, and something that never even entered my mind' — the Topheth (tōpet, possibly meaning fireplace/hearth) was the cultic installation in the Valley of Ben Hinnom (Gehenna) south of Jerusalem where child sacrifice to Molech was practiced (2 Kings 23:10; Isaiah 30:33). The practice may have been Phoenician in origin, brought to Judah through the influence of Phoenician-Sidonian culture during Ahab's era (the marriage to Jezebel) and revived under Manasseh. The fire-sacrifice of children is labeled as something YHWH never commanded and never imagined — the strongest possible statement that the practice is not a legitimate expression of covenant worship.
32: 'Therefore, the days are coming ... when this place will no longer be called Topheth or the Valley of Ben Hinnom, but the Valley of Slaughter' — the renaming is a judgment oracle: the place of intentional child-burning will become the place of mass unintentional death. 'For they will bury the dead in Topheth until there is no room left' — the irony inverts the geography of horror: the valley where Israel burned children will become the valley where Israel buries war dead. The Valley of Ben Hinnom (Gehenna) later became Jerusalem's refuse dump, burning perpetually, and the word Gehenna (from the Greek transliteration of gēʾ hinnōm) became the NT's standard term for the place of final judgment (Matthew 5:22,29; Jeremiah 10:28; Jeremiah 18:9).
33: 'The corpses of this people will become food for the birds of the sky and the wild animals of the earth, and no one will drive them away' — the unburied body is the ultimate disgrace in the ANE: proper burial was the final act of family and community obligation, and denial of burial was a curse reserved for enemies (1 Kings 21:24; 2 Kings 9:10). The 'no one to drive them away' detail is the absence of all social function: there are no surviving family members to protect the bodies, no community to maintain the minimum of human dignity in death.
34: 'I will bring to an end the sounds of joy and gladness, the voices of the bridegroom and the bride, in the cities of Judah and the streets of Jerusalem — for the land will become a wasteland' — the stopping of wedding sounds is the quintessential image of social death in the ancient world: marriage, procreation, and social celebration are the most basic human activities that continue even in adverse conditions. When weddings stop, the community has ceased to function. Jeremiah uses this image at 16:9 and Jeremiah 25:10; Revelation 18:23 applies the same image to the fall of Babylon. The 'voice of the bridegroom and bride' appears positively in 33:11 as a sign of restoration.
